unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Look up! In this unit you learn the words for everything in the big sky: the sun, the moon, the stars, the clouds and your own shadow. You find out why a shadow is long in the morning and short at midday, and why we have day and night. You collect describing words for the sky and the weather, you write true sky facts in the present simple, and you use verbs ending in -ed to tell what you saw last night. Along the way you read “The Night Amal Counted the Stars” and wonder about the world, just as Amal does.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher lecture, then learn a few sky, time and weather words each day.
Work through the six grammar practices: describing words, the present simple for true facts, and the past simple with -ed.
Read the shadow poem, the sundial text, Nora's listening text, the day and night text, and the story “The Night Amal Counted the Stars”, then answer the nineteen comprehension questions.
Complete the six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions in each of the two weeks.
Finish with the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.
unit.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 2 Unit 4 source pack under docs/lesson-authoring-super-prompt.md

visual.alt: Cartoon children in a garden watching their long shadows, with the sun, clouds, the moon and the stars shown across one day.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A dark shape made when something blocks the light.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My shadow follows me when the sun shines.
practiceSentences: Amal watched her shadow grow long on the sandy path.
Why does your shadow hide under your feet at midday?
“Stand still and look at your shadow,” said Adam.
I traced my shadow with chalk on the playground.
The tall tree makes a cool shadow for us to sit in.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - h - a - d - o - w
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Stand with your helper in the sunlight, describe your shadow out loud, and ask the tutor to check your sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The brightness that lets us see things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We need light to see things clearly.
practiceSentences: The morning light came through the kitchen window.
How much light do you need to read your book?
“Turn on the lamp, please. We need more light,” said Hana.
I like the soft light in the evening.
Without light we cannot see any colours.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to sit with you, name three things in your room that give light, and ask the tutor which one is brightest.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The star that gives Earth light and heat in the day.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sun rises in the east and sets in the west.
practiceSentences: The sun climbs higher in the sky all morning.
Is the sun really a star, Grandma Hana?
“Do not look straight at the sun,” warned Adam.
I feel the warm sun on my face.
At midday the sun is high above the school roof.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper nearby, tell the tutor two things the sun gives us, and ask it to add one more.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The place where something comes from.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sun is a source of light and heat.
practiceSentences: Our biggest source of light is the sun.
Is a torch a source of light at night?
“Name one more source of light,” said Nora.
I drew every source of light in my house.
A candle is a small source of light and heat.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - o - u - r - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask your adult helper to walk round the house with you, then tell the tutor every source of light you found.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To stop something from passing through or going by.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: When you block the light, you make a shadow.
practiceSentences: Thick clouds block the sunlight on a grey day.
What happens to your shadow if you block the lamp?
“Block the light with your hand,” said Adam.
I block the torch and my shadow appears on the wall.
Curtains block the bright morning light.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, block a lamp with your hand and ask the tutor to explain why a shadow appears.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The space above the Earth that you see when you look up.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sky is blue in the day and dark at night.
practiceSentences: A flock of birds crossed the wide sky.
What colour is the sky outside your window?
“Look up at the sky tonight,” said Grandma Hana.
I painted the sky orange and pink.
Grey clouds filled the sky before the rain.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Look out of a window with your helper, describe the sky in one sentence, and ask the tutor for a better describing word.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The early part of the day, after the sun comes up.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In the morning we eat breakfast.
practiceSentences: Every morning the sun climbs slowly into the sky.
Do you see your long shadow in the morning?
“Good morning! The sun is up already,” called Nora.
I water the garden before school each morning.
The morning air felt cool and fresh.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - o - r - n - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor three things you do every morning while an adult listens, and ask it to check your sentences.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The middle of the day, when the sun is highest.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: At midday my shadow is very short.
practiceSentences: At midday the sun stands high above our heads.
Where does your shadow hide at midday?
“It is nearly midday. Look at the clock,” said Adam.
I eat my lunch at midday with my sister Mina.
The midday sunshine felt hot on the sand.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - i - d - d - a - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: At midday, step outside with an adult, and ask the tutor why your shadow is so short.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The part of the day before night, when the sun sets.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In the evening the sky turns orange.
practiceSentences: In the evening the sun sinks low behind the hills.
What do you like doing in the evening?
“Sit with me this evening,” said Grandma Hana.
I read my book every evening before bed.
The evening light turned the clouds pink and gold.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - v - e - n - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to watch the evening sky with you, then tell the tutor two colours you can see.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The time when the sun comes up in the morning.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sky glows red and gold at sunrise.
practiceSentences: At sunrise the sky glows gold above the sea.
Have you ever watched a sunrise from your window?
“Wake up! You will miss the sunrise,” laughed Adam.
I woke early to see the sunrise with my brother.
Birds begin to sing at sunrise.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - n - r - i - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper, draw the colours of a sunrise, then ask the tutor to describe a sunrise in three words.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The time when the sun goes down in the evening.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: At sunset the sun goes down and the sky turns orange.
practiceSentences: The whole town looks golden at sunset.
What time is sunset where you live?
“Come inside soon after sunset,” said Grandma Hana.
I drew a picture of the sunset last night.
Long shadows stretch across the ground at sunset.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - n - s - e - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to tell you what time sunset is today, then ask the tutor why the sky turns red.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The bright round object we see in the night sky.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The moon shines because the sun's light falls on it.
practiceSentences: A round moon rose slowly over the hills.
Is the moon bigger than a star?
“The moon has no light of its own,” said Grandma Hana.
I can see the moon from my bedroom window.
Some nights the moon is only a thin curve.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Look for the moon with an adult tonight, then ask the tutor to explain why its shape changes.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A tiny point of light in the night sky. The sun is a star.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I cannot count all the stars in the sky.
practiceSentences: Amal counted fifty stars and then gave up.
How many stars can you see tonight?
“Each star is a sun far away,” said Grandma Hana.
I like watching the stars from our doorstep.
The stars come out when the sky grows dark.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Stand outside with an adult, count ten stars together, and ask the tutor one question about stars.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: White or grey shapes in the sky made of tiny drops of water.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A big cloud covered the sun.
practiceSentences: One white cloud drifted slowly over our house.
Can a cloud really hide the whole sun?
“That cloud looks like a boat,” laughed Nora.
I watch the clouds change shape after lunch.
Dark grey clouds bring rain to the town.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Lie on the grass with an adult, describe one cloud shape, and ask the tutor to describe a different one.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The time when your part of Earth faces the sun and it is light.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We play outside in the day.
practiceSentences: In the day we can see without a lamp.
Why is the sky blue during the day?
“The day is leaving now,” said Grandma Hana.
I feel wide awake in the middle of the day.
Our part of Earth faces the sun in the day.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult listening, explain what makes it day, and ask the tutor to check your explanation.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The time when your part of Earth faces away from the sun and it is dark.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We can see the moon and stars at night.
practiceSentences: At night our part of Earth turns away from the sun.
Do you see many stars at night?
“Good night. Sleep well,” said Grandma Hana.
I keep a small light on at night.
The garden is quiet and cool at night.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask an adult to help you list three things you see at night, then ask the tutor to add one.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Our planet. It turns round, which gives us day and night.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Earth turns slowly, round and round.
practiceSentences: Earth is turning all the time, but we cannot feel it.
How long does Earth take to turn round once?
“Our Earth is turning,” Adam explained.
I made a model of Earth from a ball.
Earth travels round the sun every year.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Spin a ball with an adult to show how Earth turns, then ask the tutor why we have day and night.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Reaching a great distance from one end to the other.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My shadow is long in the morning.
practiceSentences: In the morning my shadow is long and thin.
Is your shadow long or short right now?
“Look how long the shadows are!” said Nora.
I drew a long line for the evening shadow.
Long shadows lie across the field at sunset.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Measure your shadow with an adult in the morning, then ask the tutor why it is so long.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Reaching only a small distance from one end to the other.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: At midday my shadow is short.
practiceSentences: At midday my shadow becomes very short.
Why is a midday shadow so short?
“Your shadow is short now,” said Adam.
I stood still and looked at my short shadow.
A short shadow tells us the sun is high.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Compare your morning shadow and your midday shadow with an adult, then ask the tutor which one is short and why.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Far above the ground or above another level.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sun is high at midday.
practiceSentences: The sun is high above the school at midday.
How high can a kite fly in the sky?
“The moon is high tonight,” said Grandma Hana.
I watched a bird fly high over the roof.
When the sun is high, our shadows are short.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Point at something high in the sky with an adult, and ask the tutor to describe it in one sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Near the ground or below another level.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sun is low in the evening.
practiceSentences: In the evening the sun hangs low over the sea.
Is the sun low in the morning too?
“The sun is low, so your shadow is long,” said Adam.
I saw a low cloud sitting on the hill.
A low sun makes long shadows on the ground.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Watch the low evening sun with an adult, then ask the tutor what happens to your shadow.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Giving out or sending back a lot of light.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The bright moon lit up the whole garden.
practiceSentences: The midday sun is so bright that I close my eyes.
Which star looks brightest tonight?
“What a bright morning!” said Nora.
I wore a hat because the sunshine was bright.
A bright lamp helps me read in the evening.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - r - i - g - h - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, name the brightest thing you can see indoors, and ask the tutor to compare it with the sun.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having very little light, so it is hard to see.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The night sky is dark.
practiceSentences: The sky grew dark and the stars appeared.
Can you see colours in a dark room?
“It is getting dark. Come inside,” called Grandma Hana.
I like the dark sky because I can see stars.
Shadows are dark because light cannot pass through you.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with an adult in a dim room, describe what you can and cannot see, and ask the tutor why.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having the colour of a clear daytime sky.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sky is blue on a clear day.
practiceSentences: On a clear day the sky is bright blue.
Why does the sky look blue at midday?
“Today the sky is deep blue,” said Nora.
I coloured my sky picture blue and white.
The sea looks blue when the sky is clear.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Find three blue things at home with an adult, then ask the tutor which one matches the sky best.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having a colour between black and white.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sky is grey when it is cloudy.
practiceSentences: The sky turned grey before the rain came.
Is the sky grey or blue today?
“Those grey clouds look heavy,” said Adam.
I wore my coat under the grey sky.
Grey clouds hide the sun and take away our shadows.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Look at the clouds with an adult, decide whether they are grey or white, and ask the tutor what grey clouds mean.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having the colour of a ripe orange fruit.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The evening sky turns orange.
practiceSentences: At sunset the whole sky glows orange.
Is the sky orange or red this evening?
“Look at that orange sky!” said Nora.
I painted an orange stripe above the hills.
Orange light spread across the clouds at sunset.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: o - r - a - n - g - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Watch an orange evening sky with an adult, then ask the tutor to name two more sunset colours.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Bright with plenty of sunshine and very few clouds.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Today the weather is sunny.
practiceSentences: It is sunny, so we can play outside.
Was it sunny when you walked to school?
“What a sunny day!” said Nora.
I always see my shadow on a sunny day.
Sunny weather makes the garden warm and dry.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Look outside with an adult, decide whether it is sunny, and ask the tutor for two more weather words.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Covered with many clouds, so little sunlight comes through.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Today the weather is cloudy.
practiceSentences: It is cloudy, so I cannot see my shadow.
Does the sky look cloudy this morning?
“It is too cloudy to see the stars,” said Adam.
I like cloudy days because they are cool.
Cloudy weather often comes before the rain.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - l - o - u - d - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, count the clouds you can see, then ask the tutor whether that counts as cloudy.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: With a lot of moving air.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Today the weather is windy.
practiceSentences: It is windy, so the clouds move quickly.
Is it windy enough to fly a kite?
“Hold your hat! It is very windy,” laughed Nora.
I love windy days beside the sea.
Windy weather pushes the clouds across the sky.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Step outside with an adult, feel the moving air, and ask the tutor how we know that a day is windy.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: With rain falling, or with rain likely to fall.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Today the weather is rainy.
practiceSentences: It is rainy, so I need my raincoat.
Do you like rainy afternoons?
“A rainy day is good for reading,” said Grandma Hana.
I splashed through puddles on the rainy path.
Rainy weather helps the garden grow.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Watch the rain from a window with an adult, and ask the tutor to describe a rainy day in one sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: With little or no rain or water.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The ground is dry after a week of sunshine.
practiceSentences: The sandy path was dry and warm.
Is the washing dry yet?
“The soil is much too dry,” said Adam.
I hung my coat up to dry by the window.
Dry weather lasts for many weeks in summer.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Touch something wet and something dry with an adult, then ask the tutor to explain the difference.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name the things you can see in the sky: the sun, the moon, the stars and the clouds.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Every sky word named in the Speaking 4 sky watch recording, and all six sky words used correctly in the Writing 3 story response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say what a shadow is and explain how it forms when something blocks the light.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: The four-step explanation written in Writing 5 with a labelled diagram, plus the three shadow sentences spoken in Speaking 2.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about how the sky changes in the morning, at midday and in the evening.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Three time-ordered journal entries in Writing 2 and the matching answers in Activity 2.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Explain in simple words why we have day and night.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Correct answers to comprehension questions 10 and 11, and the ball-and-torch explanation recorded in Speaking 5.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use describing words such as long, short, high, low, bright and dark.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A different adjective in each of the four sentences in Grammar 4, and describing words in all six sentences of Writing 1.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use the present simple to say true facts about the sky.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five correct verb forms in Grammar 2 and six correctly-ended verbs in the Writing 4 fact file.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use the past simple with verbs ending in -ed to tell what you did.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four correct -ed forms in Grammar 3 and five different -ed verbs in the Writing 6 diary page.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Describe today's weather using the words sunny, cloudy, windy, rainy and dry.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: The weather word and sky description in the Speaking 6 report, plus the matching and compound-word answers in Activity 6.

readings.title: A Poem: “My Shadow”
readings.passageScript:
Read this poem slowly, then say it out loud:
I have a little shadow that goes in and out with me,
And what can be the use of him is more than I can see.
He is very, very like me from the heels up to the head,
And I see him jump before me, when I jump into my bed.
(by Robert Louis Stevenson)

readings.title: Telling Time with a Shadow
readings.passageScript:
Read this short text:
A sundial is like a clock. A clock uses hands to point at the numbers. A sundial uses a shadow. The shadow moves as the sun moves across the sky. In the morning, the shadow is long. At midday, the shadow is short. In the evening, the shadow is long again. Long ago, people used sundials to tell the time, before they had clocks.

readings.title: Nora's Shadow
readings.passageScript:
Listen to Nora. (Ask an adult or your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
“My name is Nora. My shadow changes during the day. In the morning, the sun is low, and my shadow is very long. At midday, the sun is high, and my shadow is short — it is right under my feet! In the evening, the sun is low again, and my shadow is long again.”

readings.title: Why We Have Day and Night
readings.passageScript:
Listen and follow along:
“The sun shines on our planet, Earth. Earth turns slowly, round and round. When your part of Earth faces the sun, it is day. When your part turns away from the sun, it is night. That is why the sun seems to rise in the morning and set in the evening.”

readings.title: The Night Amal Counted the Stars
readings.genre: A warm family story with dialogue.
readings.theme: The sky through one day — sun, shadows, clouds, moon and stars.
readings.setting: A small town by the sea, from early morning until late at night.
readings.passageScript:
In a small town near the sea, Amal woke up early. The morning sky was pale and pink. The sun was low. It climbed slowly into the big sky.
Amal ran outside to feed the goats. On the sandy ground she saw a long, thin shape. It moved when she moved.
“Look, Adam!” she called to her big brother. “My shadow is so long this morning!”
Adam smiled. “In the morning the sun is low,” he said. “When the sun is low, your shadow is long. Watch what happens at midday.”
By midday the sun was high and bright. Amal stood very still and looked down. Her shadow was now short. It hid right under her feet!
“It shrank!” she laughed. “Where did my long shadow go?”
“The sun is high, so your shadow is short,” said Adam. “The sun did not move by itself. Our Earth is turning, round and round, all day long.”
Amal thought about that. The whole Earth was turning, but she could not feel it. She looked up. A few white clouds floated across the blue sky. One cloud drifted over the sun. Her shadow disappeared.
“The cloud blocked the light!” said Amal. “No light, no shadow.”
“Now you understand shadows,” said Adam.
In the evening, the sky turned orange and red. The sun was low again, and Amal's shadow grew long. Their grandmother, Grandma Hana, sat outside on a mat.
“Come and sit with me, Amal,” she said softly. “The day is leaving, and the night is coming.”
Slowly the sky grew dark. One by one, tiny lights began to shine. Amal had never really looked before.
“Grandma,” she whispered, “there are so many stars!”
“More than anyone can count,” said Grandma Hana. “Each star is like our sun, but very, very far away. Our sun is only a star that is close to us.”
Amal tried to count anyway. “One, two, three, four…” But every time she looked, she found more. She reached fifty and gave up, laughing.
Then the moon rose over the hills, round and bright.
“Why does the moon shine, Grandma?” Amal asked. “Does it have its own light, like the sun?”
“No, little one,” said her grandmother. “The moon has no light of its own. The sun shines on it. The moon gives us that light at night. Is it not beautiful?”
Amal nodded. She thought about the whole big sky. She thought about the sun that changed her shadow. She thought about the clouds and the countless stars. She thought about the gentle moon.
“Grandma,” she said, “the sky is full of wonderful things. I never really looked up before.”
Grandma Hana smiled and held her hand. “Curious people wonder about the world,” she said. “They look. They ask questions. They say thank you for this beauty. Tomorrow the sun will rise, and a new day will begin.”
That night Amal fell asleep happy. In her dreams the stars were still shining. She counted them one by one across the big sky.

comprehension.question: The poet writes about his shadow as if it were a person. Which little word does he use instead of “it”?
comprehension.explanation: The poem says “what can be the use of him”, so the shadow is called him, not it.

comprehension.question: Where does the little shadow go with the poet?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It goes in and out with him everywhere, and it even goes with him into bed.
comprehension.explanation: Line one says the shadow goes in and out with him, and the last line takes it right into his bed.

comprehension.question: The poem says the shadow is “very, very like me”. From where to where is it like the poet?
comprehension.correctAnswer: From the heels up to the head, which is his whole body.
comprehension.explanation: Line three gives the two ends: the heels at the bottom and the head at the top.

comprehension.question: In the last line, what does the shadow do at the very moment the poet gets into bed?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It jumps before him, so it seems to jump into bed first.
comprehension.explanation: The poem ends with the shadow jumping ahead of the poet. Award one mark for the jump and one for saying when it happens.

comprehension.question: What does a sundial use to tell the time?
comprehension.explanation: The text says a clock uses hands, but a sundial uses a shadow that moves as the sun moves.

comprehension.question: When is the shadow on a sundial short, and when is it long?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It is short at midday, and it is long in the morning and long again in the evening.
comprehension.explanation: Three sentences in the middle of the text give the three times of day.

comprehension.question: How is a sundial the same as a clock, and how is it different?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Both tell the time. A clock uses hands that point at numbers, while a sundial uses a moving shadow and needs sunlight.
comprehension.explanation: The first two sentences give the likeness, and the third gives the difference. Award one mark for each half.

comprehension.question: When is Nora's shadow long, and when is it short?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It is long in the morning and in the evening, and it is short at midday.
comprehension.explanation: Nora describes her shadow three times, once for each part of the day.

comprehension.question: Why does Nora's shadow change during the day? Use the word sun in your answer.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because the sun is low in the morning and in the evening, and high at midday.
comprehension.explanation: Nora links every change to where the sun is, so the answer must name the sun.

comprehension.question: According to the text, what makes it day where you live, and what makes it night?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It is day when your part of Earth faces the sun, and night when your part turns away from the sun.
comprehension.explanation: The text gives both halves in two sentences that begin with the word when.

comprehension.question: The text says the sun “seems to rise”. Does the sun really travel across the sky, or is something else moving?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The sun does not travel. Earth turns slowly, so the sun only seems to rise and set.
comprehension.explanation: The second sentence says Earth turns, and the last sentence explains that this is why the sun seems to move.

comprehension.question: What colour was the sky when Amal woke up in the morning?
comprehension.explanation: The second sentence of the story describes the morning sky before the sun has climbed.

comprehension.question: Why was Amal's shadow long in the morning but short at midday?
comprehension.correctAnswer: In the morning the sun was low, so her shadow was long. At midday the sun was high, so her shadow was short.
comprehension.explanation: Adam explains the rule twice, once for the morning and once for midday.

comprehension.question: What happened to Amal's shadow when a cloud drifted over the sun, and why?
comprehension.correctAnswer: It disappeared for a moment, because the cloud blocked the light.
comprehension.explanation: Amal says it herself: “No light, no shadow.” The cloud stopped the sunlight reaching her.

comprehension.question: Who sat outside with Amal in the evening and told her about the stars and the moon?
comprehension.explanation: She sits down on a mat as the sky turns orange and stays with Amal until the moon rises.

comprehension.question: Why does the moon shine, according to Grandma Hana?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The moon has no light of its own. The sun shines on it, and the moon gives that light back to us at night.
comprehension.explanation: Grandma Hana answers Amal's question in the paragraph just after the moon rises.

comprehension.question: Amal says she “never really looked up before”. Have you looked at the night sky? Say what you saw, or what you would like to see.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Any personal answer that names at least one real thing in the night sky, such as the moon, a star or a cloud.
comprehension.explanation: This question links Amal's discovery to the child's own night sky. Ask them to add one describing word.

comprehension.question: Grandma Hana says that curious people look, ask questions and say thank you for beautiful things. What in nature would you say thank you for?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Any personal answer that names something in nature and gives one reason for choosing it.
comprehension.explanation: The answer should point at something real, such as rain, trees or the sea, and say why it matters to the child.

comprehension.question: Amal could not feel the Earth turning under her feet. What other things happen around us that we cannot feel or see?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Any sensible example, such as air moving, plants growing, sound travelling or a heart beating.
comprehension.explanation: Amal is amazed that the whole Earth turns without her feeling it. Ask the child to explain why we cannot feel their example either.

grammar.title: Describing Words (Adjectives)
grammar.explanation: An adjective is a describing word. It tells us more about a noun, which is a person, a place or a thing. Adjectives help you paint a clear picture of the sky, the sun and your own shadow.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
An adjective usually comes just before its noun, or after the words is and are.
For shadows we use long and short: “My shadow is long in the morning.”
For the sun we use high and low: “The sun is high at midday.”
For light we use bright and dark: “The day is bright. The night is dark.”
For the sky we use blue, grey and orange: “The evening sky is orange.”
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “The sky is colour blue.” The word colour is not needed, so the sentence becomes “The sky is blue.”
grammar.memoryTip: An adjective answers the question “What is it like?” If your sentence sounds plain, add one describing word and the picture appears.
grammar.practice:
Choose a good adjective from the box to finish each sentence. Box: high, long, dark, blue.
1. At midday, the sun is ______ in the sky.
2. In the morning, my shadow is ______.
3. At night, the sky is ______.
4. On a clear day, the sky is ______.

grammar.title: Describing Words: Paint the Sky
grammar.explanation: Now you choose the describing words yourself. A good writer picks the adjective that fits exactly what they can see, so the reader pictures the same sky.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Pick one describing word for each part of your sky picture.
The morning sky was pale and pink.
One thin, grey cloud crossed the bright sun.
My shadow looked long and narrow on the wall.
The evening sky turned deep orange.
grammar.commonMistake: It is easy to use the same word twice, as in “The big sun is in the big sky.” Change one of them, so the sentence becomes “The bright sun is in the wide sky.”
grammar.memoryTip: Before you write, look up and ask three questions: what size, what colour, and how much light?
grammar.practice: Look out of your window and write four sentences about the sky. Use a different describing word in each sentence, and underline every one you used.

grammar.title: Present Simple (True Facts)
grammar.explanation: We use the present simple for facts and for things that are always true. The sky is full of facts, so this is the perfect tense for talking about it.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the plain verb with I, you, we and they.
I see the moon. We look at the sky. They make shadows.
Add -s or -es with he, she and it. The sun, the moon and a shadow are all “it”.
The sun rises in the east. A shadow forms when light is blocked. The Earth turns slowly.
grammar.commonMistake: Children often write “The sun rise in the east.” The sun is an “it”, so the verb needs its -s: “The sun rises in the east.”
grammar.memoryTip: Say it like this: he, she and it always want the s. The sun is an “it”, so we write “The sun shines.”
grammar.practice:
Write each verb in the present simple.
1. The sun ______ (rise) in the east.
2. Shadows ______ (change) during the day.
3. The moon ______ (shine) at night.
4. The Earth ______ (turn) round and round.
5. We ______ (see) stars in the night sky.

grammar.title: Present Simple: Sky Fact File
grammar.explanation: A fact file uses the present simple, because everything in it is always true. In this task you write your own sky facts and then check every verb ending.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Match the verb ending to who or what is doing the action.
The Earth turns once every day.
Stars shine in the night sky.
One thick cloud blocks the sunlight.
We see the moon at night.
grammar.commonMistake: Writing “Stars shines at night.” adds an -s that is not needed. Stars means more than one, so the verb stays plain: “Stars shine at night.”
grammar.memoryTip: One thing takes the s on the verb, and many things leave the verb plain. One star shines, but many stars shine.
grammar.practice: Write six true sky facts in the present simple. Write one fact each about the sun, the moon, stars, clouds, shadows and the Earth. Circle every verb and check its ending.

grammar.title: Past Simple (verbs ending in -ed)
grammar.explanation: We use the past simple to tell what has already happened, such as yesterday, last night or last week. Many verbs make the past simple by adding -ed.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Add -ed to most verbs to talk about the past.
look becomes looked: “Last night I looked at the stars.”
watch becomes watched: “We watched the sun set.”
play becomes played: “Yesterday I played in the sunshine.”
The word stays the same for everyone. I, you, he, she, we and they all use looked.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write “Yesterday I look at the moon.” The word yesterday tells us it has finished, so add -ed: “Yesterday I looked at the moon.”
grammar.memoryTip: If it has already happened, listen for the -ed at the end: played, watched, counted.
grammar.practice:
Write each verb in the past simple by adding -ed.
1. Last night we ______ (look) at the moon.
2. Yesterday the children ______ (watch) the clouds.
3. This morning I ______ (play) outside in the sun.
4. Amal ______ (count) the stars last night.

grammar.title: Past Simple: My Sky Diary
grammar.explanation: A diary tells what has already happened, so every verb in it needs the past simple. In this task you write about a sky that you have really seen.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with a time word, then use your -ed verb.
Last night I watched the moon rise.
Yesterday morning I traced my long shadow.
After lunch we counted seven clouds.
At sunset the sky turned deep orange.
grammar.commonMistake: A time word and a present verb do not match, as in “Last night I watch the stars.” Once you write last night, the verb must become watched.
grammar.memoryTip: Time word first, then -ed. If your sentence starts with yesterday or last night, the action is already finished.
grammar.practice: Write five diary sentences about a sky you have seen. Begin each one with a time word such as yesterday, last night or this morning, and use a different -ed verb in every sentence.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Describe the Sky
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Look out of your window right now and get ready to speak.
Say “It is the ______.” and choose morning, midday or evening.
Say “The sky is ______.” and choose blue, grey or orange.
Say “The sun is ______, and my shadow is ______.” using high or low, and long or short.
Record yourself, then check: did you name the time of day, a sky colour, and where the sun is?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with an adult, describe the sky you can see in three sentences, and ask the tutor which describing word was the strongest.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Explain How a Shadow Forms
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Practise explaining shadows to someone at home.
Say “A shadow forms when something blocks the light.”
Say “The shadow is on the dark side, away from the sun.”
Say “Shadows are dark because light cannot pass through.”
Then use your hand and a lamp to show what you have just explained.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, explain how a shadow forms, and ask the tutor whether your explanation is correct and complete.

speaking.title: Speaking Game — Shadow Play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play this game near a sunny window or a lamp, with an adult or a friend.
Make a shape with your hands and say “My hands are making a shadow.”
Say what it looks like, such as “It looks like a bird.” or “It looks like an elephant.”
Move your hands and say “When I move, the shadow moves too!”
Ask the other person to guess your shape, then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Describe your hand shadow to the tutor while an adult watches, and let the tutor guess the shape before you say it.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Sky Watch Report
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Watch the sky three times in one day and report what you saw.
Begin each report with the time: “In the morning…”, “At midday…”, “In the evening…”.
Name at least two things you can see each time, such as the sun, the clouds, the moon or the stars.
Record all three reports one after the other, and speak slowly enough for a listener to picture your sky.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Read your three sky reports to the tutor with an adult nearby, and ask it which report gave the clearest picture.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Shadow Scientist
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Be a scientist and explain why we have day and night.
Use a ball for Earth and a torch for the sun, and ask an adult to hold the torch.
Say “When my part of Earth faces the sun, it is day.”
Say “When my part turns away from the sun, it is night.”
Record your explanation while you turn the ball slowly.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Explain day and night to the tutor using your ball and torch, with an adult holding the torch, and ask it for one more fact.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — Weather Presenter
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Present today's weather like a television presenter.
Start with “Good morning. Here is today's weather.”
Say whether it is sunny, cloudy, windy or rainy, and describe the sky above you.
Finish with one sentence about what people should wear or take with them.
Record your report and play it to your family.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Perform your weather report for the tutor while an adult listens, and ask it to suggest one extra weather word.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Describe Your Shadow
writing.promptAndInstructions: Go outside on a sunny day and look carefully at your shadow. Finish these four sentences, then write two more of your own. My shadow is ______. My shadow moves when I ______. My shadow is on the ______ side of me. I like my shadow because ______.
writing.modelText: My shadow is long and thin. My shadow moves when I hop. My shadow is on my dark side, away from the sun. I like my shadow because it copies everything I do. It grows tall in the evening. At midday it almost disappears.
writing.successCriteria: I finished all four sentences, added two of my own, used a describing word in each one, and ended every sentence with a full stop.
writing.support: Say each sentence out loud before you write it, and choose your describing word from Group 4 of your word list.
writing.extension: Add one sentence that uses because, such as “My shadow is short at midday because the sun is high.”

writing.title: Writing 2 — A Sky Journal
writing.promptAndInstructions: Look at the sky three times in one day and write about each one. Use these openers: In the morning, the sky is ______ and the sun is ______. At midday, the sky is ______ and my shadow is ______. In the evening, the sky is ______ and the sun is ______. Then draw the sky at each time of day and label your three pictures.
writing.modelText: In the morning, the sky is pale pink and the sun is low. At midday, the sky is bright blue and my shadow is short. In the evening, the sky is orange and the sun is low again.
writing.sentenceStarter: In the morning, the sky is
writing.successCriteria: My three sentences are in time order, each one names a sky colour, and each drawing carries a label.
writing.support: Use the colour words blue, grey, orange and pink from your word list.
writing.extension: Add a fourth sentence about the night sky that names the moon and the stars.

writing.title: Writing 3 — Story Response: The Night Amal Counted the Stars
writing.promptAndInstructions: Answer three tasks about the story. First write three sentences about the sky today, beginning each one with “Today the sky is…”. Then write two sentences about the moon and the stars at night. Finally draw Amal looking up at the stars with Grandma Hana, and write one sentence under your picture.
writing.modelText:
Today the sky is bright blue. Today the sky is full of white clouds. Today the sky is windy and cool.
The moon is round and bright tonight. I can see many tiny stars around it.
Amal is counting the stars with Grandma Hana.
writing.successCriteria: I wrote three sky sentences, two night sentences and one caption, and every sentence has a capital letter and a full stop.
writing.support: Read the last four paragraphs of the story again before you write your caption.
writing.extension: Write what Amal might say to Grandma Hana on the following morning.

writing.title: Writing 4 — My Sky Fact File
writing.promptAndInstructions: Make a fact file about the big sky. Write six true facts in the present simple, one each about the sun, the moon, a star, a cloud, a shadow and the Earth. Give your fact file a title, and use two subheadings: the day sky and the night sky.
writing.modelText:
The Big Sky Fact File
The day sky
The sun rises in the east and sets in the west. One thick cloud blocks the sunlight and hides our shadows. A shadow forms when something stops the light.
The night sky
The moon shines because the sun's light falls on it. A star is a sun that is very far away. The Earth turns slowly, which gives us day and night.
writing.expectedLength: A title, 2 subheadings and 6 fact sentences
writing.successCriteria: Every fact is true, written in the present simple, and placed under the right subheading.
writing.support: Check every verb: one thing takes -s, and many things leave the verb plain.
writing.extension: Add one fact that you found out for yourself, and say where you learned it.

writing.title: Writing 5 — How a Shadow Forms
writing.promptAndInstructions: Explain in writing how a shadow forms. Write four sentences in order: what you need, what happens to the light, where the shadow appears, and how the shadow changes during the day. Draw a labelled diagram beside your explanation.
writing.modelText: You need a light and something solid to stop it. When my body blocks the sunlight, the light cannot pass through me. A dark shape appears behind me, away from the sun. My shadow is long in the morning and short at midday. In the evening it grows long again.
writing.successCriteria: My four sentences are in the right order, my diagram is labelled, and I used the words light, block and shadow.
writing.support: Try it with a torch and your hand first, then write down exactly what you saw.
writing.extension: Explain what happens to your shadow when a cloud covers the sun.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Last Night I Looked Up
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a short diary page about a sky that you have already seen. Write five sentences in the past simple, and begin each one with a time word such as yesterday, last night, this morning or after lunch. Use a different verb ending in -ed in every sentence.
writing.modelText: Last night I looked at the moon from my window. Yesterday morning I traced my long shadow with chalk. After lunch we watched three white clouds drift past. This morning I counted the birds crossing the sky. Last week the sky turned deep orange at sunset.
writing.successCriteria: Every sentence begins with a time word and uses a different verb ending in -ed.
writing.support: List your five -ed verbs before you start writing: looked, watched, played, counted and traced.
writing.extension: Add one sentence that says how the night sky made you feel, and why.

activities.title: Activity 1 — True or False (Sky Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each sky fact. If it is false, write the correct sentence underneath.
1. The sun rises in the east and sets in the west.
2. The sun is highest in the sky at midnight.
3. The moon shines because the sun's light falls on it.
4. Clouds can block the sunlight, so shadows do not form.
activities.answerSummary: 1. True. 2. False, the sun is highest at midday. 3. True. 4. True.

activities.title: Activity 2 — Match the Time to the Shadow
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Draw two columns and match each time of day to the right sun and shadow. Then read each pair aloud as one sentence.
Times of day: morning, midday, evening
Sun and shadow: the sun is high and the shadow is short; the sun is low and the shadow is long; the sun is low again and the shadow is long again
activities.answerSummary: Morning goes with the sun is low and the shadow is long. Midday goes with the sun is high and the shadow is short. Evening goes with the sun is low again and the shadow is long again.

activities.title: Activity 3 — Fill in the Blanks
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write out each sentence in full and fill the gap.
1. Right now the weather is ______ (sunny, cloudy, windy or rainy).
2. We see the ______ and the ______ in the sky at night.
3. At midday my shadow is ______ (long or short).
4. The sky looks ______ (name the colour) right now.
activities.answerSummary: 1. The child's real weather word. 2. moon, stars. 3. short. 4. The child's real sky colour, such as blue or grey.

activities.title: Activity 4 — Shadow Measure
activities.instructionsAndItems: Ask an adult to help you measure your shadow three times on a sunny day. Stand in the same spot each time, mark the end of your shadow with a stone, and measure with a tape or by counting your own footsteps. Record the morning, midday and evening lengths in a small table, then write one sentence saying when your shadow was longest.
activities.answerSummary: The morning and evening shadows are the longest and the midday shadow is the shortest. Accept any unit, including footsteps, as long as all three measurements use the same one.

activities.title: Activity 5 — Day or Night Sort
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort these eight things into two columns headed We see it in the day and We see it at night: the sun, the moon, the stars, a bright blue sky, your shadow, a dark sky, sunrise, moonlight.
activities.answerSummary: Day: the sun, a bright blue sky, your shadow, sunrise. Night: the moon, the stars, a dark sky, moonlight.

activities.title: Activity 6 — Weather and Compound Word Match
activities.instructionsAndItems:
There are two tasks here. First match each weather word to its clue: sunny, cloudy, windy, rainy, dry.
Clues: a bright sky with no clouds; a sky full of grey clouds; a kite pulling hard on its string; drops falling on a window; ground that has had no rain for weeks.
Then break each compound word into its two little words: sunshine, sunrise, sunset, raincoat.
activities.answerSummary: sunny goes with a bright sky with no clouds. cloudy goes with a sky full of grey clouds. windy goes with a kite pulling hard on its string. rainy goes with drops falling on a window. dry goes with ground that has had no rain for weeks. The compounds split as sun and shine, sun and rise, sun and set, rain and coat.

assignments.title: The Big Sky observation portfolio
assignments.instructions: Put together three pieces of work from this unit. First finish “Writing 2 — A Sky Journal” with all three drawings labelled. Next record “Speaking 4 — Sky Watch Report”, describing those same three skies out loud. Finally finish “Writing 6 — Last Night I Looked Up” and check that every verb ends in -ed. Read all three against the writing and speaking rubrics before you send them in together.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does the word shadow mean?
quizzes.options: The time when the sun goes down in the evening. | White or grey shapes in the sky made of tiny drops of water. | A dark shape made when something blocks the light. | The brightness that lets us see things.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A dark shape made when something blocks the light.
quizzes.explanation: A shadow needs something solid to stop the light. The other three choices describe sunset, clouds and light.

quizzes.question: Which of these gives Earth its light and heat in the day?
quizzes.options: the sun | the evening | a raincoat | grey
quizzes.explanation: The sun is the star closest to us, and it gives Earth both light and heat. Evening is a time, a raincoat is clothing, and grey is a colour.

quizzes.question: Which of these is the time when your part of Earth faces away from the sun?
quizzes.options: a cloud | night | a source | windy
quizzes.explanation: When your part of Earth has turned away from the sun, it is night. A cloud is a sky object, a source is where something comes from, and windy describes the weather.

quizzes.question: The sun is a ____ of light and heat.
quizzes.explanation: A source is the place something comes from, so the sun is our biggest source of light. A shadow is dark, midday is a time, and a cloud blocks the light.

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses a describing word correctly?
quizzes.options: The sky is colour blue. | The sky is blue. | The sky blue is. | The sky is the blue.
quizzes.explanation: The describing word blue comes straight after is. The word colour is not needed, and the other two sentences are in the wrong order.

quizzes.question: Which sentence is written correctly in the present simple?
quizzes.options: The sun rise in the east. | The sun rises in the east. | The sun rising in the east. | The sun to rise in the east.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The sun rises in the east.
quizzes.explanation: The sun is an “it”, so its verb takes -s. The other three sentences have the wrong verb form.

quizzes.question: Yesterday I ____ at the moon for a long time.
quizzes.explanation: Yesterday tells us the action is already finished, so the verb adds -ed. Look and looks are present forms, and looking needs a helping word.

quizzes.question: The sky is full of grey clouds and we cannot see the sun. What is the weather like?
quizzes.explanation: Cloudy means many clouds cover the sky and hide the sun. Orange is a colour, midday is a time, and sunny is the opposite of what the question describes.

quizzes.question: The compound word sunrise is made from two little words. Which two?
quizzes.options: sun + rise | sea + side | rain + coat | moon + light
quizzes.explanation: Sunrise splits into sun and rise. The other three joins make seaside, raincoat and moonlight.

quizzes.question: At midday the sun is high above you. What is your shadow like?
quizzes.options: long and thin, stretching across the ground | orange and bright, like an evening sky | short, right under your feet | wet and cold, like a rain cloud
quizzes.explanation: A high sun makes a short shadow that hides under your feet. Long shadows belong to the morning and the evening, and shadows are never orange or wet.

liveSessions.title: The Big Sky: sky words and the first shadow watch
liveSessions.beforeSession: Watch the teacher lecture and learn Group 1 of the word list: shadow, light, sun, source and block.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome; 10 min sky-word retrieval with picture cards; 10 min torch and hand shadow demonstration; 15 min Speaking 2 in pairs; 5 min reflection
liveSessions.afterSession: Record Speaking 1 — Describe the Sky and upload it.

liveSessions.beforeSession: Read the story once at home and underline every sky word that you meet.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min story recap; 15 min shared reading aloud with attention to speech marks; 15 min story questions 12 to 16; 10 min hot-seating Amal and Grandma Hana
liveSessions.afterSession: Answer the three Think and talk questions in your book.

liveSessions.title: Grammar workshop: describing words for the sky
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read Grammar Lesson 1 and try its practice on your own.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min adjective warm-up; 10 min adjectives before the noun and after is; 15 min describing a sky photograph in pairs; 10 min writing four sky sentences; 5 min sharing
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete Grammar 4 and underline every describing word you used.

liveSessions.title: Sundials, day and night: the science of the sky
liveSessions.beforeSession: Reread “Telling Time with a Shadow” and “Why We Have Day and Night”.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min quick quiz on shadows; 15 min ball and torch model of day and night; 15 min building a paper sundial; 10 min explaining it in pairs

liveSessions.title: Guided writing: sky journals and sky facts
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring your sky journal from Writing 2 with all three drawings labelled.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min journal share; 10 min present-simple verb endings; 20 min guided writing of the sky fact file; 10 min partner proofreading
liveSessions.afterSession: Finish Writing 4 — My Sky Fact File and check every verb ending.

liveSessions.title: The Big Sky: weather showcase and review
liveSessions.beforeSession: Practise the weather report that you prepared for Speaking 6.

liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.

teacherNotes.noteType: AI tutor use and device safety
teacherNotes.note: Every AI tutor task in this unit assumes an adult is present. Never leave a child alone with the device: sit beside them, keep the screen visible, and close the session together. The tutor prompts from the source pack are grouped here by file. Vocabulary: “Why do shadows change during the day?”, “Why is the sky usually blue in the day?”, “Is the sun a star? Can you tell me about stars?”, “Can you quiz me on the weather words?” Grammar: “Can you check my grammar answers?”, “Why do we say the sun rises but I rise?”, “Can you give me five more sentences to practise the past simple?” Reading and story: “Can you read the shadow poem with me and explain it?”, “Why do we have day and night? Can you explain it in an easy way?”, “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?” Writing: “Can you check if my sky journal sentences are correct?”, “Can you help me use more describing words?” Add one safety line before every outdoor task: we never look straight at the sun.

Say it at the start and repeat it on every sunny day: we never look straight at the sun, not even for a moment, and never through a window, a mirror or a lens. Shadow work needs only a bright window or a torch, so a child who cannot go outside can do every task indoors. If measuring their own shadow is difficult, let the child measure the shadow of a bottle or a stick on the windowsill instead. When marking, treat a missing verb ending as the next step rather than as a mistake: praise the child for choosing the right tense, then point at the -s or the -ed. The story carries the value of this unit, which is curiosity: keep asking questions about the world, and notice what is beautiful in it.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. True. 2. False, the sun is highest at midday, not at midnight. 3. True. 4. True, a cloud blocks the sunlight, so no shadow forms.
1. high, 2. long, 3. dark, 4. blue. Accept any word from the box that makes a true sentence about the child's own sky.
1. rises, 2. change, 3. shines, 4. turns, 5. see. Remind the child that the sun, the moon and the Earth are each an “it”, so their verbs take -s.
1. looked, 2. watched, 3. played, 4. counted. Every verb simply adds -ed, with no other spelling change.
Him. The poet writes about the shadow as if it were a person, so he says him and not it.
In and out with him everywhere he goes, and right into his bed at the end of the poem.
From the heels up to the head. Both ends are needed for the full answer.
The shadow jumps before him. Award one mark for the jump and one for saying that it happens as he jumps into bed.
A shadow. Do not accept hands, which is what a clock uses.
Short at midday, and long in the morning and again in the evening. All three times are needed.
Same: both tell the time. Different: a clock uses hands pointing at numbers, while a sundial uses a moving shadow and needs sunlight. One mark for each half.
Long in the morning and in the evening, short at midday. Nora says her midday shadow is right under her feet.
Because the sun is low in the morning and evening and high at midday. The word sun must appear in the answer.
Day when your part of Earth faces the sun, night when your part turns away from it. Both halves are needed.
The Earth turns, and the sun only seems to move. Reject an answer that says the sun travels across the sky.
Pale and pink. Accept either word on its own, but praise the child who gives both.
A low sun in the morning made a long shadow, and a high sun at midday made a short one. Look for the link between the sun and the shadow.
It disappeared, because the cloud blocked the light. Amal's own words are “No light, no shadow.”
Her grandmother, Grandma Hana. Accept grandmother on its own.
It has no light of its own. The sun shines on the moon, and the moon gives that light back to us at night.
Personal answer. Award the mark when the child names at least one real thing in the night sky and adds one describing word.
Personal answer. Award the mark when the child names something in nature and gives one reason for choosing it.
Accept any sensible example, such as air moving, plants growing, sound travelling or a heart beating, with a short reason why we cannot feel it.
A dark shape made when something blocks the light. The other options describe sunset, clouds and light.
the sun. It is the star closest to Earth, and it gives us both light and heat.
night. Night is the time when your part of Earth has turned away from the sun.
source. A source is where something comes from, and our light and heat come from the sun.
The sky is blue. The describing word follows is, and the word colour is not needed.
Morning goes with a low sun and a long shadow, midday with a high sun and a short shadow, and evening with a low sun and a long shadow again.
Four sky sentences, each using a different describing word, with every one underlined. Reject a sentence that repeats an adjective already used.
Six true facts, one each about the sun, the moon, stars, clouds, shadows and the Earth. Singular subjects take -s on the verb, and plural subjects leave it plain.
Five diary sentences, each opening with a time word and using a different -ed verb, for example looked, watched, played, counted and traced.
Three reports in one recording, each opening with the time of day and naming at least two things in the sky. Mark clarity and detail, not the weather itself.
Listen for both key sentences: facing the sun makes day, and turning away makes night. The ball must keep turning slowly while the child speaks.
A weather report with the greeting, one weather word, a description of the sky, and one piece of advice about what to wear or take.
A titled fact file with two subheadings and six true facts in the present simple, three under the day sky and three under the night sky.
Four sentences in order: what is needed, the light being blocked, where the shadow appears, and how it changes during the day. The diagram must be labelled.
Five past-simple sentences, each starting with a time word and using a different -ed verb. Check that no verb has been left in the present.
The morning and evening measurements are the longest and the midday one is the shortest. All three must use the same unit, including footsteps.
Day: the sun, a bright blue sky, your shadow, sunrise. Night: the moon, the stars, a dark sky, moonlight.

sunny with the bright cloudless sky, cloudy with the sky full of grey clouds, windy with the kite, rainy with the drops on the window, dry with the ground that has had no rain. Compounds: sun and shine, sun and rise, sun and set, rain and coat.
The sun rises in the east. The sun is an “it”, so its verb takes -s.
looked. Yesterday tells us the action is finished, so the verb adds -ed.
cloudy. Many grey clouds cover the sky and hide the sun.
sun + rise. The other joins make seaside, raincoat and moonlight.
short, right under your feet. A high midday sun makes the shortest shadow of the whole day.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name the sun, the moon, the stars and the clouds.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what a shadow is and how it forms.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can talk about the sky in the morning, at midday and in the evening.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can explain in simple words why we have day and night.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use describing words like long, short, high and low.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the present simple and the past simple to talk about the sky.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

rubrics.level3: Mostly clear and easy to follow

rubrics.level1: Few sky or weather words used
rubrics.level2: Some sky words, usually after a prompt
rubrics.level3: Most sky, time and weather words used correctly
rubrics.level4: Wide range of unit words chosen with care

rubrics.level2: Verb endings correct after a reminder
rubrics.level3: Present simple mostly correct, with adjectives in place
rubrics.level4: Present and past simple always correct, with well chosen adjectives

rubrics.level1: Needs full support to take a turn
rubrics.level4: Takes turns, listens and adds a new idea

rubrics.level1: Ideas unclear or away from the sky topic
rubrics.level2: One sky idea with little detail
rubrics.level3: Clear sky ideas with some detail
rubrics.level4: Clear sky ideas with well chosen detail

rubrics.level2: Some time order, with gaps in the sequence
rubrics.level3: Sentences in a sensible time order
rubrics.level4: Strong order using time words, subheadings or labels

rubrics.level3: Good use of sky, time and weather words
rubrics.level4: Precise adjectives that paint a clear picture

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and full stops mostly missing
rubrics.level2: Some capital letters and full stops
rubrics.level3: Capital letters and full stops usually correct
rubrics.level4: Accurate punctuation and correct -s and -ed verb endings
